question_title: What will the 10-year U.S. Treasury constant maturity yield be on June 25, 2026?
summary: The 10-year Treasury yield on June 25, 2026 is forecast to center around 4.49%, slightly above the current ~4.47% level. The distribution reflects: (1) a starting point of 4.47% from the latest FRED/market data, (2) the FOMC meeting June 16-17 creating event risk with an expected shift from easing bias to neutral stance (mildly hawkish), (3) 7 business days of random walk volatility based on empirical daily std of ~4.1 bps, and (4) scenario-weighted FOMC outcomes (65% base case, 18% hawkish surprise, 12% dovish surprise, 5% very hawkish). The Monte Carlo simulation with 500K paths produces a median of 4.489% with P10-P90 range of 4.34-4.64%, appropriately wider than pure random walk due to FOMC tail risk. The US-Iran deal reducing energy prices is already partially reflected in the recent yield decline.

factors.description: Current yield at ~4.47%, anchoring the distribution center near the latest observed value
FOMC expected to hold rates and shift to neutral stance - mildly hawkish for longer yields, pushing center slightly above 4.47
Hawkish surprise scenario (18% prob): Warsh signals inflation concern, aggressive SEP, yields jump to 4.55-4.65 area
Dovish surprise scenario (12% prob): Warsh more accommodative than expected, yields drop to 4.35-4.45 area
US-Iran deal reduces energy/inflation pressures, already partially reflected in recent yield decline from 4.55 to 4.45
Recent regime mean ~4.505% provides mild mean-reversion pull slightly above current level
Daily volatility of ~4.1 bps over 7 trading days creates ~10.8 bps base random walk uncertainty (symmetric)

reasoning:
## Forecast Summary: 10-Year Treasury Yield on June 25, 2026

**Median estimate: 4.49%** (P10: 4.34%, P90: 4.64%)

### Key Findings

| Factor | Impact |
|--------|--------|
| Current yield ~4.47% | Strong anchor for distribution center |
| FOMC neutral stance shift | Mildly hawkish → slight upward pull (+1-2 bps median) |
| Hawkish surprise risk (18%) | Right-tail skew toward 4.55-4.65% |
| Dovish surprise risk (12%) | Left-tail possibility toward 4.35-4.45% |
| US-Iran deal | Already reflected in recent decline from 4.55 to 4.45 |
| 7-day random walk volatility | ~10.8 bps σ from empirical daily vol of 4.1 bps |

### Methodology
- Monte Carlo simulation (500K paths) with scenario-weighted FOMC outcomes
- Empirical daily volatility from the post-May-20 stable regime (17 observations)
- Four FOMC scenarios: base/neutral (65%), hawkish (18%), dovish (12%), very hawkish (5%)
- Distribution center at 4.49% reflects the slight hawkish tilt from the expected easing-to-neutral stance shift
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) What will the 10-year U.S. Treasury constant maturity yield be on June 25, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/44068)

[US 10 Year Treasury Note YieldQuote - Chart - Historical Data - News](https://tradingeconomics.com/united-states/government-bond-yield)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves to the 10-year U.S. Treasury constant maturity yield (FRED series DGS10) reported for the observation date June 25, 2026, expressed in percent. For example, a yield of 4.45% resolves as 4.45. The value can be found at https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/DGS10 .
fine_print: The resolving value is the daily closing yield published by FRED / the Federal Reserve H.15 release for the observation dated June 25, 2026, as displayed on the DGS10 series page (https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/DGS10). If June 25, 2026 is a non-business day or no value is published for that date (e.g., a holiday or missing observation), the most recent available DGS10 observation dated on or before June 25, 2026 will be used. The value is taken as reported by the source. If FRED is unavailable, the equivalent 10-Year Treasury constant maturity figure from the Federal Reserve's H.15 release (https://www.federalreserve.gov/releases/h15/) for the same date will be used.

factors.description: Current yield at ~4.47%, anchoring the center of the distribution
FOMC expected to hold rates and shift to neutral stance - mildly hawkish, pushing yields slightly higher
Hawkish surprise scenario (18% prob): Warsh signals inflation concern, aggressive SEP
Dovish surprise scenario (12% prob): Warsh more accommodative than expected
US-Iran deal reduces energy/inflation pressures - already partially reflected in recent yield decline
Recent regime mean ~4.505% with std ~0.045% provides mean-reversion anchor slightly above current level
Daily volatility of ~4.1 bps over 7 trading days creates ~10.8 bps random walk uncertainty

input.anchor: Current yield of 4.47-4.48% as of June 15-16, 2026. Over recent regime (since May 20), mean is 4.505% with daily std of 4.5 bps.
input.assessment:
The 10-year Treasury yield resolves on June 25, 2026, about 7 business days away. Current level is ~4.47%. The key event is the FOMC meeting June 16-17, the first chaired by Kevin Warsh. Markets overwhelmingly expect a rate hold but anticipate a shift from easing bias to neutral stance, which is mildly hawkish for yields.

The Monte Carlo simulation with scenario-weighted FOMC outcomes produces a median of 4.489% with P10=4.34% and P90=4.64%. The distribution is slightly right-skewed due to hawkish tail scenarios. The center is pulled slightly above the current yield due to the expected neutral stance shift.

Key uncertainty is the FOMC communication - Warsh's first press conference could move markets in either direction. The US-Iran deal reducing energy prices is a dovish factor but appears already reflected in the recent decline from 4.55 to 4.45 area.

The model's P10-P90 range of ~30 bps is appropriately wider than the pure random-walk range (~28 bps) due to FOMC event risk.
input.calibration_notes: The distribution width is grounded in empirical daily volatility (4.1 bps) scaled by sqrt(7 days) = 10.8 bps for the random walk component, plus FOMC event risk adding ~3-5 bps of additional uncertainty. The P10-P90 range of 30 bps is consistent with recent monthly trading range of ~29 bps (4.38-4.67). The slight upward bias from current level reflects the expected hawkish policy stance shift.
input.key_uncertainties: 1. FOMC communication tone - Warsh's first press conference is the single largest uncertainty driver. 2. Whether the neutral stance shift is already priced in or will move markets. 3. Any unexpected economic data releases between now and June 25.
input.tool_audit: FRED DGS10 data: excellent, provided full recent history and regime stats. CNBC quotes: useful for real-time cross-validation. FXStreet FOMC preview: good for scenario framing. TradingEconomics: confirmed current yield. Web search: provided good contextual information on FOMC expectations and US-Iran deal.
input.process_reflection: This question is well-suited to quantitative modeling. The FRED data with regime stats was very helpful. The scenario-based Monte Carlo approach naturally handles the FOMC event risk. The main friction was getting the very latest data point (June 13/15/16) - FRED lags by a day or two, so I had to triangulate with market quotes. Overall the tooling worked well for this type of financial time-series forecast.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Verification Summary

### Evidence Grounding — All 7 Factors Verified

**Factor 1** (Current yield ~4.47%): FRED DGS10 shows 4.48 on June 12 (latest FRED data). CNBC shows 4.477% intraday. TradingEconomics confirms 4.48% on June 16. DealPlexus shows 4.47%. The ~4.47% claim is well-supported. ✓

**Factor 2** (FOMC hold + neutral stance shift): FXStreet article confirms "FOMC is widely expected to hold the federal funds rate steady at 3.50% to 3.75%" and "A shift from an easing bias to a neutral policy stance is anticipated." April 2026 minutes already showed majority favoring dropping easing bias. ✓

**Factor 3** (Hawkish surprise 18%): The trace shows Warsh "publicly questioned [dot plot's] usefulness," Yahoo Finance mentions bond market "betting that interest rates need to rise," and CNBC references CPI at "highest inflation since 2023." The scenario description is a reasonable tail-risk construction from these sources. ✓

**Factor 4** (Dovish surprise 12%): Reasonable counterfactual scenario. The trace doesn't provide specific evidence that Warsh will be dovish, but the factor is correctly low-confidence (0.3) and appropriately weights this possibility. ✓

**Factor 5** (US-Iran deal): TradingEconomics confirms "the US and Iran agreed to a deal that softened inflationary risks" and "Pro-inflationary risks dropped as oil and fuel prices retreated further." The claim that it's "already partially reflected" is supported by the yield decline from 4.55 to 4.45 area. ✓

**Factor 6** (Regime mean ~4.505%): FRED regime_stats confirm: stable_since "2026-05-20", stable_mean 4.505294, stable_std 0.044868, 17 observations. ✓

**Factor 7** (Daily volatility ~4.1 bps): Execute_code output confirms "Std daily change: 0.0408" for the regime since May 20. The 10.8 bps random walk std over 7 days (0.041 × √7 ≈ 0.1085) is verified by simulation output. ✓

### Double-Counting
Factors 1 (current level) and 6 (regime mean) draw on the same FRED DGS10 data, but serve different analytical purposes — one as a point anchor, the other as a mean-reversion target. Mild overlap but not distortive. Factors 2-4 are mutually exclusive FOMC scenarios, so no double-counting.

### Wrong-Direction Factors
All logit signs are consistent: Factor 5 (Iran deal dovish, logit=-0.3) correctly pushes yield down. Factors 2, 3, 6 (hawkish/mean-reversion-up) correctly have positive logits. Factor 7 (random walk uncertainty, logit=0.0) is correctly neutral. ✓

### Resolution Criteria Alignment
The agent correctly targets FRED DGS10 for June 25, 2026, uses the correct data source, and notes the fallback to the most recent business day if needed. The fine print is properly engaged. ✓

### Regime-Spanning Data
The agent computed volatility from the post-May-20 regime only (17 observations), as identified by FRED's regime_stats. The prior regime (mean 4.64) is excluded. No contamination. ✓

### Counterargument Quality
Strong. Identifies: (1) Warsh reshaping Fed communication framework, (2) inflation data pushing toward rate hikes, (3) oil price collapse scenario, (4) FOMC tail risk understated because historical volatility excludes FOMC days. Provides specific quantitative triggers for updating (10-15 bps, 5-10 bps, 15-20 bps shifts). This is genuine adversarial reasoning. ✓

### Confidence Assessment
0.55 is appropriate for a 7-day horizon with a major FOMC meeting creating genuine directional uncertainty. ✓

### Probability Assessment
The Monte Carlo output (median 4.489, P10 4.339, P90 4.643) is consistent with: starting value 4.47, 7 business days of random walk (σ_daily ≈ 4.1 bps), plus FOMC event risk with scenario weighting (65% neutral/+1bp drift, 18% hawkish/+8bp, 12% dovish/-6bp, 5% very hawkish/+14bp). The ~2bp upward shift in median vs. starting value is justified by the expected neutral-stance shift being mildly hawkish. The P10-P90 width of 30.4 bps exceeds the pure random-walk 80% CI of 27.8 bps by the expected amount for FOMC event risk. My independent estimate would center at 4.48-4.50 with similar P10-P90 range. No meaningful disagreement. ✓

### Tool Audit
Agent claims no tool failures; ground-truth metrics confirm 19 calls, 0 errors (0.0% error rate). Consistent. ✓

### Worldview Consistency
The worldview research entries (from May 2026) show 10Y yield at 4.43% on May 5, with yields having risen from 4.05% in early March. The current June 12 reading of 4.48% is consistent with the ongoing range-bound behavior within the post-Iran-deal regime. No contradictions. ✓

No material errors found. The forecast is well-grounded in trace evidence, methodologically sound, and the probability estimate is appropriate.
